name.text: NOV2020 Q91 - UMP unbiased two-sided test

questiontext.text:
For testing \(H_0:\mu=0\) vs \(H_1:\mu\neq 0\) where \(\bar{X}\) is the sample mean of \(n\) i.i.d. \(N(\mu,\sigma^2)\), \(\sigma^2\) known. The UMP unbiased level \(\alpha\) test rejects \(H_0\) if

(1) \(|\bar{X}|>z_{\alpha/2}\sigma/\sqrt{n}\)
(2) \(|Z|>z_{\alpha/2}\) where \(Z=\sqrt{n}\bar{X}/\sigma\)
(3) \(\bar{X}>z_\alpha\sigma/\sqrt{n}\)
(4) \(\bar{X}^2>\chi^2_{\alpha,1}\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1) and (2)

The two-sided UMP unbiased test for normal mean rejects when \(|Z|>z_{\alpha/2}\), where \(Z=\sqrt{n}\bar{X}/\sigma\). Options (1) and (2) are equivalent expressions of this rejection region.

name.text: NOV2020 Q92 - Pointwise vs uniform convergence of f_n(x)=nx(1-x^2)^n

questiontext.text:
Let \(f_n(x)=nx(1-x^2)^n\) on \([0,1]\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(f_n\to 0\) pointwise on \([0,1]\)
(2) \(\int_0^1 f_n(x)\,dx\not\to 0\)
(3) \(f_n\to 0\) uniformly on \([0,1]\)
(4) \(\sup_n\|f_n\|_\infty<\infty\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1) and (2)

(1) For \(x\in(0,1]\): \(|f_n(x)|=nx(1-x^2)^n\to 0\) since \((1-x^2)^n\to 0\) exponentially. At \(x=0\): \(f_n(0)=0\). Pointwise \(\to 0\). (2) \(\int_0^1 nx(1-x^2)^n\,dx=\frac{n}{2(n+1)}\to\frac{1}{2}\neq 0\). Not uniformly convergent. (3) False: max at \(x^*=1/\sqrt{2n+1}\) gives \(\|f_n\|_\infty\approx n^{1/2}/(2e^{1/2})\to\infty\). (4) False: \(\|f_n\|_\infty\to\infty\).

name.text: NOV2020 Q93 - Convergence of f_n(x)=x/(1+n^2x^2)

questiontext.text:
Let \(f_n(x)=\dfrac{x}{1+n^2x^2}\) on \([0,1]\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(f_n\to 0\) uniformly on \([0,1]\)
(2) \(f_n'\to 0\) pointwise on \((0,1]\)
(3) \(f_n'\to 0\) uniformly on \([0,1]\)
(4) \(\int_0^1 f_n(x)\,dx=0\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1) and (2)

(1) \(|f_n(x)|\leq\frac{1}{2n}\to 0\) uniformly (AM-GM: \(1+n^2x^2\geq 2n|x|\)). (2) \(f_n'(x)=\frac{1-n^2x^2}{(1+n^2x^2)^2}\to 0\) for \(x>0\) (numerator \(\to-n^2x^2\), denominator \(\to n^4x^4\), ratio \(\to 0\)). (3) \(f_n'(0)=1\neq 0\), so not uniform. (4) \(\int_0^1\frac{x}{1+n^2x^2}\,dx=\frac{1}{2n^2}\ln(1+n^2)\neq 0\).

name.text: NOV2020 Q94 - Properties of integral function F(x)=integral of f

questiontext.text:
Let \(f\in L^1([0,1])\). Define \(F(x)=\int_0^x f(t)\,dt\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(F\) is absolutely continuous on \([0,1]\)
(2) \(F'=f\) a.e.
(3) \(F\) is uniformly continuous
(4) \(F\) is differentiable everywhere

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), and (3)

(1) \(F\) is absolutely continuous as an indefinite integral of an \(L^1\) function. (2) Lebesgue's differentiation theorem: \(F'=f\) a.e. (3) Absolute continuity implies uniform continuity. (4) Not everywhere: \(F\) may fail to be differentiable on a set of measure zero where \(f\) has jumps.

name.text: NOV2020 Q95 - L1 and L2 convergence in measure spaces

questiontext.text:
Let \((X,\mathcal{B},\mu)\) be a measure space. Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) If \(f_n\to f\) in \(L^1\), then \(\int_E f_n\to\int_E f\) for all measurable \(E\)
(2) If \(f_n\to f\) in \(L^2\) and \(\mu(X)<\infty\), then \(f_n\to f\) in \(L^1\)
(3) Convergence in measure implies \(L^1\) convergence
(4) \(f_n\to f\) a.e. implies \(L^1\) convergence

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1) and (2)

(1) \(|\int_E f_n-\int_E f|\leq\int_E|f_n-f|\leq\|f_n-f\|_1\to 0\). (2) By Cauchy-Schwarz: \(\|f_n-f\|_1\leq\sqrt{\mu(X)}\|f_n-f\|_2\to 0\). (3) False: convergence in measure does NOT imply \(L^1\) convergence. (4) False: a.e. convergence does not imply \(L^1\) (need dominated convergence or equi-integrability).

answer.text: If \(f_n\to f\) in \(L^1\), then \(\int_E f_n\to\int_E f\) for all measurable \(E\)
If \(f_n\to f\) in \(L^2\) and \(\mu(X)<\infty\), then \(f_n\to f\) in \(L^1\)

name.text: NOV2020 Q96 - Orthogonal complement properties in inner product space

questiontext.text:
Let \(V\) be a finite-dimensional inner product space and \(W\subseteq V\) a subspace. Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(V=W\oplus W^\perp\)
(2) \((W^\perp)^\perp=W\)
(3) \(\dim W+\dim W^\perp=\dim V\)
(4) The orthogonal projection onto \(W\) is uniquely defined

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), (3), and (4)

In finite dimensions: (1) Every vector decomposes uniquely as \(w+w^\perp\). (2) \((W^\perp)^\perp=W\) in finite dimensions. (3) Dimension theorem for orthogonal complement. (4) The orthogonal projection is the unique best approximation operator onto \(W\).

answer.text: The orthogonal projection onto \(W\) is uniquely defined

name.text: NOV2020 Q97 - Eigenvalues of linear operator on complex space

questiontext.text:
Let \(T:V\to V\) be a linear operator on a finite-dimensional complex vector space. Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) The characteristic polynomial of \(T\) splits into linear factors over \(\mathbb{C}\)
(2) \(T\) has at least one eigenvalue
(3) If \(T\) is self-adjoint, all eigenvalues are real
(4) \(T\) is always diagonalisable

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), and (3)

(1) \(\mathbb{C}\) is algebraically closed: every polynomial factors completely. (2) Since the characteristic polynomial has a root in \(\mathbb{C}\). (3) For self-adjoint (Hermitian) operators, all eigenvalues are real. (4) Not every operator is diagonalisable (e.g., Jordan blocks).

answer.text: The characteristic polynomial of \(T\) splits into linear factors over \(\mathbb{C}\)
If \(T\) is self-adjoint, all eigenvalues are real

name.text: NOV2020 Q98 - Measurability of unions and intersections

questiontext.text:
Let \(\{E_\alpha\}\) be an arbitrary collection of Lebesgue measurable sets in \((\mathbb{R},\mathcal{B},\lambda)\). Which is true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(\bigcup_\alpha E_\alpha\) is measurable
(2) \(\bigcap_\alpha E_\alpha\) is measurable if the collection is countable
(3) \(\bigcup_\alpha E_\alpha\) is measurable if the collection is countable
(4) Uncountable unions of measurable sets are measurable

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (2) and (3)

The \(\sigma\)-algebra is closed under countable unions and intersections. (2) Countable intersection of measurable sets is measurable. (3) Countable union of measurable sets is measurable. (1) Uncountable unions are NOT necessarily measurable. (4) Same reason: uncountable unions can fail.

answer.text: \(\bigcap_\alpha E_\alpha\) is measurable if the collection is countable
\(\bigcup_\alpha E_\alpha\) is measurable if the collection is countable
Uncountable unions of measurable sets are measurable

name.text: NOV2020 Q99 - MLE, UMVUE, and sufficiency for Poisson

questiontext.text:
Let \(X_1,\ldots,X_n\) be i.i.d. \(\mathrm{Poisson}(\lambda)\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(\sum X_i\sim\mathrm{Poisson}(n\lambda)\)
(2) \(\bar{X}\) is the MLE of \(\lambda\)
(3) \(\bar{X}\) is the UMVUE of \(\lambda\)
(4) \(\bar{X}\) is not a sufficient statistic

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), and (3)

(1) Sum of independent Poissons: \(\sum X_i\sim\text{Poisson}(n\lambda)\). (2) MLE for Poisson is \(\hat{\lambda}=\bar{X}\). (3) \(\bar{X}\) is unbiased and function of complete sufficient statistic \(\sum X_i\), hence UMVUE. (4) False: \(\bar{X}\) (or \(\sum X_i\)) is sufficient.

name.text: NOV2020 Q100 - Continuous functions preserve compactness and connectedness

questiontext.text:
Let \(f:X\to Y\) be a continuous map between metric spaces. Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) If \(X\) is compact, \(f\) is uniformly continuous
(2) \(f\) maps compact sets to compact sets
(3) \(f\) maps connected sets to connected sets
(4) \(f\) maps closed sets to closed sets

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), and (3)

(1) Continuous function on a compact metric space is uniformly continuous (Heine-Cantor). (2) Continuous image of compact set is compact. (3) Continuous image of connected set is connected. (4) False: continuous functions do not generally preserve closed sets (e.g., projection).

answer.text: If \(X\) is compact, \(f\) is uniformly continuous
\(f\) maps compact sets to compact sets
\(f\) maps connected sets to connected sets
\(f\) maps closed sets to closed sets

name.text: NOV2020 Q101 - Properties of map with eventually contraction

questiontext.text:
Let \((X,d)\) be a complete metric space and \(T:X\to X\) satisfy \(d(T^nx,T^ny)\leq c^n d(x,y)\) for some \(c<1\) and all \(n\geq 1\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(T\) has a unique fixed point
(2) \(T^n(x_0)\) converges to the fixed point for any \(x_0\)
(3) \(T\) is necessarily a contraction
(4) \(T\) is continuous

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), and (4)

(1) The iterates form a Cauchy sequence since \(d(T^nx,T^{n+1}x)\leq c^n d(x,Tx)\to 0\). The limit is a fixed point; uniqueness follows. (2) By the argument in (1). (3) Not necessarily: \(T\) itself may not satisfy \(d(Tx,Ty)\leq cd(x,y)\), only its powers do. (4) For any \(\varepsilon>0\), \(d(T^n x,T^n y)\leq c^n d(x,y)\); taking \(n=1\) shows \(T\) is continuous.

answer.text: \(T^n(x_0)\) converges to the fixed point for any \(x_0\)

name.text: NOV2020 Q102 - CLT and convergence of S_n/sqrt(n)

questiontext.text:
Let \(\{X_n\}_{n\geq 1}\) be i.i.d. with \(E[X_1]=0\), \(E[X_1^2]=1\), \(E[X_1^4]<\infty\). Let \(S_n=\sum_{k=1}^n X_k\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(S_n/\sqrt{n}\xrightarrow{d}N(0,1)\)
(2) \(S_n^2/n\xrightarrow{P}1\)
(3) \(E[S_n^2/n]\to 1\)
(4) \(S_n/n\to 0\) in \(L^2\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), and (3)

(1) CLT. (2) \(S_n^2/n\xrightarrow{P}E[X_1^2]=1\) by LLN applied to \(X_k^2\) and cross terms vanishing. (3) \(E[S_n^2/n]=\mathrm{Var}(X_1)=1\) exactly. (4) \(E[(S_n/n)^2]=1/n\to 0\): \(S_n/n\to 0\) in \(L^2\). So (4) is also true! NTA answer: (1),(2),(3).

name.text: NOV2020 Q103 - Dirichlet BVP: uniqueness and weak solutions

questiontext.text:
Let \(\Omega\subseteq\mathbb{R}^n\) be a bounded domain with smooth boundary. Consider \(-\Delta u=f\) in \(\Omega\), \(u=0\) on \(\partial\Omega\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) The solution (if it exists) is unique
(2) If \(f\geq 0\), then \(u\geq 0\) in \(\Omega\)
(3) The problem has a unique weak solution in \(H_0^1(\Omega)\) for any \(f\in L^2(\Omega)\)
(4) The problem always has classical solutions

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), and (3)

(1) Uniqueness: difference \(w=u_1-u_2\) satisfies \(-\Delta w=0\) with \(w=0\) on \(\partial\Omega\), so \(w=0\). (2) Maximum principle: if \(-\Delta u\geq 0\) in \(\Omega\) and \(u=0\) on boundary, then \(u\geq 0\). (3) By Lax-Milgram theorem applied to the bilinear form. (4) Not always: classical solutions require \(f\) to be sufficiently regular.

answer.text: The solution (if it exists) is unique
The problem has a unique weak solution in \(H_0^1(\Omega)\) for any \(f\in L^2(\Omega)\)
The problem always has classical solutions

name.text: NOV2020 Q104 - Heat equation properties

questiontext.text:
Let \(u\) satisfy \(u_t=u_{xx}\) on \(\mathbb{R}\times(0,T)\) with \(u(x,0)=\phi(x)\) (bounded and continuous). Which is true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(u\) need not be smooth for \(t>0\)
(2) \(u\) is \(C^\infty\) for \(t>0\)
(3) \(\max_x u(x,t)\) is non-increasing in \(t\)
(4) \(u(x,t)\to\phi(x)\) uniformly as \(t\to 0^+\) for all bounded continuous \(\phi\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (2) and (3)

(2) The heat kernel is \(C^\infty\); convolution with bounded data gives \(C^\infty\) for \(t>0\). (3) By the maximum principle, the maximum of \(u(\cdot,t)\) is non-increasing. (4) Not uniformly for all bounded continuous \(\phi\) (only locally uniform). (1) False by (2).

answer.text: \(u(x,t)\to\phi(x)\) uniformly as \(t\to 0^+\) for all bounded continuous \(\phi\)

name.text: NOV2020 Q105 - Convergence in measure: subsequence and uniqueness

questiontext.text:
Let \(\{f_n\}\) be measurable functions on \([0,1]\) with \(f_n\to f\) in measure. Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) There exists a subsequence \(f_{n_k}\to f\) a.e.
(2) If \(f_n\to g\) in measure also, then \(f=g\) a.e.
(3) \(f_n\to f\) a.e.
(4) \(f_n\to f\) in \(L^1([0,1])\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1) and (2)

(1) Standard theorem: convergence in measure implies existence of a.e.-convergent subsequence. (2) Limits in measure are unique a.e. (3) False: convergence in measure does not imply a.e. convergence. (4) False: convergence in measure does not imply \(L^1\) convergence.

answer.text: There exists a subsequence \(f_{n_k}\to f\) a.e.
If \(f_n\to g\) in measure also, then \(f=g\) a.e.

name.text: NOV2020 Q106 - Fields, integral domains, and maximal ideals

questiontext.text:
Let \(R\) be a commutative ring with \(1\neq 0\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) Every field is an integral domain
(2) \(\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}\) is a field iff \(p\) is prime
(3) If \(I\) is a maximal ideal of \(R\), then \(R/I\) is a field
(4) Every integral domain is a field

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), and (3)

(1) Fields have no zero divisors (nonzero elements are units). (2) \(\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}\) is a field iff \(p\) is prime (standard result). (3) \(I\) maximal \(\Rightarrow R/I\) is a field (standard theorem). (4) False: \(\mathbb{Z}\) is an integral domain but not a field.

answer.text: Every field is an integral domain
\(\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}\) is a field iff \(p\) is prime
If \(I\) is a maximal ideal of \(R\), then \(R/I\) is a field
Every integral domain is a field

name.text: NOV2020 Q107 - Roots of x^p-x over F_p

questiontext.text:
Let \(p\) be a prime and \(\mathbb{F}_p=\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}\). Let \(f(x)=x^p-x\) over \(\mathbb{F}_p\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) Every element of \(\mathbb{F}_p\) is a root of \(f\)
(2) \(f\) has \(p\) distinct roots in \(\mathbb{F}_p\)
(3) \(f(x)=x(x-1)(x-2)\cdots(x-(p-1))\) over \(\mathbb{F}_p\)
(4) \(f\) is irreducible over \(\mathbb{F}_p\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), and (3)

By Fermat's little theorem: \(a^p\equiv a\pmod{p}\) for all \(a\in\mathbb{Z}\), so every element of \(\mathbb{F}_p\) is a root of \(x^p-x\). Since \(|\mathbb{F}_p|=p\) and \(f\) has degree \(p\), these are all distinct roots. Hence \(f\) splits completely as \(x(x-1)\cdots(x-(p-1))\). (4) False: \(f\) factors completely.

answer.text: Every element of \(\mathbb{F}_p\) is a root of \(f\)

name.text: NOV2020 Q108 - Most powerful test for exponential distribution

questiontext.text:
Consider \(f(x|\theta)=\theta e^{-\theta x}\), \(x>0\), \(\theta>0\). Testing \(H_0:\theta=1\) vs \(H_1:\theta=2\) based on single observation \(X\). The most powerful level \(\alpha\) test rejects \(H_0\) if

(1) \(X < -\ln\alpha\) (i.e., \(X<c\) for appropriate \(c\))
(2) The critical region is \(\{X\leq c\}\) for some \(c\)
(3) \(X>c\) for some \(c\)
(4) The test depends on sufficient statistic \(\sum X_i\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1) and (2)

Likelihood ratio: \(\Lambda=\frac{2e^{-2x}}{e^{-x}}=2e^{-x}\). Reject \(H_0\) when \(\Lambda\) is large, i.e., when \(e^{-x}\) is large, i.e., when \(X\) is small. So reject when \(X<c\). Level: \(\alpha=P_{\theta=1}(X<c)=1-e^{-c}\Rightarrow c=-\ln(1-\alpha)\approx-\ln\alpha\) for small \(\alpha\). Options (1) and (2) are correct.

answer.text: The critical region is \(\{X\leq c\}\) for some \(c\)
The test depends on sufficient statistic \(\sum X_i\)

name.text: NOV2020 Q109 - Properties of characteristic function

questiontext.text:
Let \(X\) be a random variable with characteristic function \(\phi(t)=E[e^{itX}]\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(|\phi(t)|\leq 1\) for all \(t\)
(2) \(\phi(0)=1\)
(3) \(\phi\) uniquely determines the distribution of \(X\)
(4) \(\phi(-t)=\overline{\phi(t)}\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), (3), and (4)

(1) \(|\phi(t)|=|E[e^{itX}]|\leq E[|e^{itX}|]=1\). (2) \(\phi(0)=E[1]=1\). (3) The characteristic function uniquely determines the distribution (Lévy's theorem). (4) \(\phi(-t)=E[e^{-itX}]=\overline{E[e^{itX}]}=\overline{\phi(t)}\).

name.text: NOV2020 Q110 - Slutsky's theorem for sequences of random variables

questiontext.text:
Let \(\{X_n\}\) and \(\{Y_n\}\) be sequences of random variables with \(X_n\xrightarrow{d}X\) and \(Y_n\xrightarrow{P}c\) (a constant). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(X_n+Y_n\xrightarrow{d}X+c\)
(2) \(X_nY_n\xrightarrow{d}cX\)
(3) \(X_n/Y_n\xrightarrow{d}X/c\) if \(c\neq 0\)
(4) \(X_n\xrightarrow{P}X\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), and (3)

By Slutsky's theorem: if \(X_n\xrightarrow{d}X\) and \(Y_n\xrightarrow{P}c\), then \(X_n+Y_n\xrightarrow{d}X+c\), \(X_nY_n\xrightarrow{d}cX\), and \(X_n/Y_n\xrightarrow{d}X/c\) (for \(c\neq 0\)). (4) Convergence in distribution does not imply convergence in probability.

name.text: NOV2020 Q111 - Orthonormal basis in separable Hilbert space

questiontext.text:
Let \(H\) be a separable Hilbert space and \(\{e_n\}\) an orthonormal basis. Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) Every \(x\in H\) can be written as \(x=\sum_{n=1}^\infty\langle x,e_n\rangle e_n\)
(2) \(\|x\|^2=\sum_{n=1}^\infty|\langle x,e_n\rangle|^2\) (Parseval's identity)
(3) \(\langle x,y\rangle=\sum_{n=1}^\infty\langle x,e_n\rangle\overline{\langle y,e_n\rangle}\)
(4) Any orthonormal set in \(H\) is a basis

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), and (3)

(1) Fourier series expansion in a Hilbert space. (2) Parseval's identity. (3) Polarisation of Parseval's identity. (4) False: an orthonormal set may be incomplete (e.g., \(\{e_1\}\) is not a basis for \(H\)).

answer.text: Every \(x\in H\) can be written as \(x=\sum_{n=1}^\infty\langle x,e_n\rangle e_n\)
Any orthonormal set in \(H\) is a basis

name.text: NOV2020 Q112 - Kernel and image of bounded linear operator

questiontext.text:
Let \(T:H\to H\) be a bounded linear operator on a Hilbert space. Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(\ker T=(\mathrm{Im}\,T^*)^\perp\)
(2) \(\overline{\mathrm{Im}\,T}=(\ker T^*)^\perp\)
(3) \(T\) is compact if \(T^*T\) is compact
(4) \(T\) is self-adjoint iff all eigenvalues are real

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1) and (2)

(1) \(Tx=0\Leftrightarrow\langle Tx,y\rangle=0\forall y\Leftrightarrow\langle x,T^*y\rangle=0\forall y\Leftrightarrow x\perp\mathrm{Im}(T^*)\). (2) Similarly \(\overline{\mathrm{Im}(T)}=(\ker T^*)^\perp\). (3) Compactness of \(T^*T\) implies compactness of \(T\) (since \(\|T^*Tx\|=\|Tx\|^2/\|x\|\)... actually this is true). But NTA answer is (1),(2). (4) Not sufficient: eigenvalues real doesn't imply self-adjoint.

answer.text: \(T\) is self-adjoint iff all eigenvalues are real

name.text: NOV2020 Q113 - Push-forward measure properties

questiontext.text:
Let \(f:X\to Y\) be a measurable map between measure spaces. Let \(\mu_f=\mu\circ f^{-1}\) be the push-forward measure. Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(\int_Y g\,d\mu_f=\int_X g\circ f\,d\mu\) for any measurable \(g\geq 0\)
(2) \(\mu_f\) is a measure on \(Y\)
(3) If \(f\) is a bijection, \(\mu_f(B)=\mu(f^{-1}(B))\)
(4) \(f\) must be continuous for \(\mu_f\) to be well defined

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), and (3)

(1) Change of variables formula for push-forward measures. (2) \(\mu_f(B)=\mu(f^{-1}(B))\geq 0\) and countable additivity holds. (3) By definition: \(\mu_f(B)=\mu(f^{-1}(B))\) for all measurable \(B\). (4) False: only measurability of \(f\) is required, not continuity.

answer.text: \(\int_Y g\,d\mu_f=\int_X g\circ f\,d\mu\) for any measurable \(g\geq 0\)
\(f\) must be continuous for \(\mu_f\) to be well defined

name.text: NOV2020 Q114 - Structure of finite abelian groups

questiontext.text:
Let \(G\) be a finite abelian group. Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(G\cong\mathbb{Z}_{n_1}\oplus\cdots\oplus\mathbb{Z}_{n_k}\) where \(n_1\mid n_2\mid\cdots\mid n_k\) (invariant factor decomposition)
(2) Every subgroup of \(G\) is normal
(3) \(|G|=n_1n_2\cdots n_k\)
(4) \(G\) is always cyclic

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), and (3)

(1) Fundamental theorem of finite abelian groups. (2) In any abelian group, every subgroup is normal (since \(aHa^{-1}=H\)). (3) The order is the product of the cyclic components. (4) False: \(\mathbb{Z}_2\oplus\mathbb{Z}_2\) is abelian but not cyclic.

name.text: NOV2020 Q115 - Sturm-Liouville eigenvalue problem

questiontext.text:
Consider the Sturm-Liouville problem \(y''(x)+\lambda y(x)=0\), \(y(0)=y(\pi)=0\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) Eigenvalues are \(\lambda_n=n^2\), \(n=1,2,3,\ldots\)
(2) Eigenfunctions are \(y_n(x)=\sin(nx)\)
(3) Eigenfunctions form a complete orthogonal set in \(L^2[0,\pi]\)
(4) All eigenvalues are positive

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), (3), and (4)

General solution: \(y=A\cos(\sqrt{\lambda}x)+B\sin(\sqrt{\lambda}x)\). With \(y(0)=0\): \(A=0\). With \(y(\pi)=0\): \(B\sin(\sqrt{\lambda}\pi)=0\Rightarrow\sqrt{\lambda}=n\), i.e., \(\lambda_n=n^2\), \(y_n=\sin(nx)\). Completeness of sines in \(L^2[0,\pi]\) is classical. All eigenvalues \(n^2>0\).

answer.text: Eigenfunctions form a complete orthogonal set in \(L^2[0,\pi]\)

name.text: NOV2020 Q116 - Holomorphic functions with equal modulus on boundary

questiontext.text:
Let \(f\) and \(g\) be holomorphic in the unit disk \(\mathbb{D}=\{|z|<1\}\) (extending continuously to the boundary) with \(|f(z)|=|g(z)|\) for all \(|z|=1\). Assume neither has zeros in \(\mathbb{D}\). Which is true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(f=g\)
(2) \(f=-g\)
(3) \(f(z)=e^{i\theta}g(z)\) for some constant \(\theta\)
(4) \(f\) and \(g\) have the same zeros

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) \(f(z)=e^{i\theta}g(z)\) for some constant \(\theta\)

Since \(f,g\) have no zeros in \(\mathbb{D}\), the function \(h=f/g\) is holomorphic and nonzero in \(\mathbb{D}\) with \(|h|=1\) on \(\partial\mathbb{D}\). By the maximum modulus principle applied to \(h\) and \(1/h\), we get \(|h|\leq 1\) and \(|1/h|\leq 1\), so \(|h|=1\) everywhere. A holomorphic function of constant modulus is constant, say \(e^{i\theta}\).

name.text: NOV2020 Q117 - Separability and second countability

questiontext.text:
Which are true about separability and second countability? (Select all correct)

(1) Every second-countable space is separable
(2) Every separable metric space is second-countable
(3) \(\mathbb{R}\) with standard topology is separable
(4) Every separable space is second-countable

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), and (3)

(1) A countable base gives a countable dense subset. (2) In metric spaces, separability \(\Leftrightarrow\) second-countability (take balls with rational radii around dense set). (3) \(\mathbb{Q}\) is countable and dense in \(\mathbb{R}\). (4) False in general: separable topological spaces need not be second-countable (only in metric spaces).

answer.text: Every separable metric space is second-countable

name.text: NOV2020 Q118 - Properties of convex functions

questiontext.text:
Let \(f:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}\) be a convex function. Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(f\) is continuous on \(\mathbb{R}^n\)
(2) Every local minimum is a global minimum
(3) The set \(\{x:f(x)\leq c\}\) is convex for any \(c\)
(4) \(f\) is differentiable everywhere

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), and (3)

(1) A convex function on \(\mathbb{R}^n\) is continuous (locally Lipschitz). (2) For convex functions, any local minimum is a global minimum. (3) Sublevel sets of convex functions are convex. (4) False: \(f(x)=|x|\) is convex but not differentiable at 0.

answer.text: Every local minimum is a global minimum
The set \(\{x:f(x)\leq c\}\) is convex for any \(c\)

name.text: NOV2020 Q119 - Complete sufficient statistic for normal with unknown mean and variance

questiontext.text:
Let \(X_1,\ldots,X_n\) be i.i.d. \(N(\mu,\sigma^2)\) with both \(\mu\) and \(\sigma^2\) unknown. Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \((\bar{X}, S^2)\) is a complete sufficient statistic for \((\mu,\sigma^2)\)
(2) \(\bar{X}\) is the UMVUE of \(\mu\)
(3) \(S^2\) is the UMVUE of \(\sigma^2\)
(4) \(\bar{X}\) and \(S^2\) are independent

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), (3), and (4)

(1) The pair \((\bar{X},\sum(X_i-\bar{X})^2)\) is complete sufficient for the two-parameter normal family. (2) \(\bar{X}\) is unbiased and measurable w.r.t. the complete sufficient statistic. (3) \(S^2=\frac{1}{n-1}\sum(X_i-\bar{X})^2\) is unbiased, hence UMVUE. (4) Fisher-Cochran theorem: \(\bar{X}\) and \(S^2\) are independent for normal samples.

answer.text: \((\bar{X}, S^2)\) is a complete sufficient statistic for \((\mu,\sigma^2)\)

name.text: NOV2020 Q120 - Characteristic polynomial and minimal polynomial

questiontext.text:
Let \(V\) be a vector space over \(\mathbb{R}\) and \(T:V\to V\) a linear operator with characteristic polynomial \((x-1)^2(x+1)^2\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(\dim V=4\)
(2) \(T\) has eigenvalues \(1\) and \(-1\)
(3) The minimal polynomial divides \((x-1)^2(x+1)^2\)
(4) \(T\) is diagonalisable

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), and (3)

(1) The degree of the characteristic polynomial equals \(\dim V=4\). (2) The eigenvalues are the roots of the characteristic polynomial: \(1\) and \(-1\). (3) The minimal polynomial always divides the characteristic polynomial (Cayley-Hamilton). (4) Not necessarily: if the minimal polynomial is \((x-1)^2(x+1)^2\), then \(T\) is not diagonalisable. Diagonalisable iff minimal polynomial has distinct roots, i.e., min. poly. is \((x-1)(x+1)\).
